name: Tianlong
description: The Tianlong, or Celestial Dragon, is a powerful and revered dragon in Chinese mythology. It is considered one of the four spiritual creatures, alongside the Qilin, Fenghuang, and Gui (turtle). The Tianlong is often depicted as a large, serpentine dragon with scales shimmering like jade or gold. It is associated with the heavens and is believed to be responsible for guarding the palaces of the gods and pulling their chariots. Unlike other dragons that control weather or earthly elements, the Tianlong is primarily a symbol of power, nobility, and divine authority. It represents the emperor and the celestial realm, embodying righteousness and justice. Its presence is often seen as a good omen, signifying prosperity and peace. The Tianlong's influence extends to various aspects of Chinese culture, from art and literature to architecture and philosophy. The creature's image is used to inspire awe and respect, reflecting the importance of the celestial order in Chinese cosmology. The Tianlong is a symbol of ultimate authority and protection, a benevolent force ensuring the balance between the mortal and divine realms. It is also said that the Tianlong can fly without wings, propelled by its sheer spiritual power and connection to the heavens. Different depictions exist throughout history, sometimes showing it with multiple heads or additional limbs, each variation adding to its majestic and awe-inspiring image. The Tianlong is a true guardian of the celestial realm, a dragon of unparalleled power and grace.
subtitle: Celestial Dragon of Divine Authority
appearance: The Tianlong is typically depicted as a large, serpentine dragon with a long, flowing body covered in shimmering scales. These scales are often described as jade-green or golden, reflecting its connection to the celestial realm. It has a majestic head with prominent horns, piercing eyes that radiate wisdom and power, and a long, flowing beard. Its claws are sharp and formidable, and it often has cloud-like formations swirling around its body, symbolizing its ability to traverse the heavens. Some depictions show it with multiple heads or additional limbs, further emphasizing its divine nature and power.
habitat: The Tianlong resides primarily in the celestial realm, guarding the palaces of the gods and pulling their chariots through the heavens. It is also sometimes depicted inhabiting sacred mountains or floating among the clouds, always in close proximity to the divine.
behavior: The Tianlong is generally benevolent and just, acting as a guardian of the celestial order. It is known for its wisdom, righteousness, and unwavering loyalty to the gods. While it possesses immense power, it rarely intervenes directly in mortal affairs, preferring to maintain balance and protect the divine realm from threats. It is a creature of immense patience and understanding, embodying the virtues of a wise and powerful ruler.

keyMyths.title: Guardians of the Celestial Palaces
keyMyths.description: The Tianlong are tasked with guarding the palaces of the gods in the celestial realm, ensuring no evil spirits or rebellious deities can disrupt the divine order. Their presence provides security and symbolizes the unwavering power of the heavens.

keyMyths.title: Pulling the Divine Chariots
keyMyths.description: The Tianlong are often depicted pulling the chariots of important deities, such as the Jade Emperor, across the sky. This task highlights their strength, loyalty, and their crucial role in maintaining the celestial order.
keyMyths.source: Classic of Mountains and Seas and other mythological collections

sources.author: Yang Lihui
sources.description: Comprehensive overview of Chinese mythological creatures and figures.
